article.title: Firefox 149 Ships Free Built-in VPN with 50 GB Monthly Limit as Mozilla Bets on Privacy to Win Back Users
article.summary: Mozilla releases Firefox 149 with a free browser-level VPN, split view, and 46 security fixes, marking its biggest feature update since the AI kill switch in version 148.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Mozilla released Firefox 149 on March 24, 2026, delivering the browser's most feature-dense update in months. The headline addition is a free built-in VPN that routes browser traffic through a secure proxy, giving users 50 GB of monthly protected browsing without requiring a separate download or extension. The release also introduces split view for side-by-side tab browsing, tab notes, and a security advisory addressing 46 vulnerabilities, over half of which are rated high severity.

The update arrives just one month after Firefox 148 introduced a [master AI kill switch](/article/2026-02/21-firefox-148-ships-a-master-ai-kill-switch-after-users-revolt-against-mozillas-ai-browser-pivot) in response to user backlash, suggesting Mozilla is now pivoting from damage control to aggressive feature development in its campaign to differentiate Firefox from Chromium-based competitors.

## What We Know

The built-in VPN uses a secure proxy server that routes only browser traffic, concealing the user's IP address and location, [according to BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/firefox-now-has-a-free-built-in-vpn-with-50gb-monthly-data-limit/). Unlike Mozilla's commercial VPN product, which covers system-wide traffic, the new free tier is limited to the browser window. Users receive 50 GB of monthly data and can selectively activate VPN protection on up to five specific websites to conserve their allowance. The feature is rolling out gradually to users in the United States, United Kingdom, Germany, and France.

Mozilla has stated that it collects only technical data for maintaining service performance, noting that the routing server is based in the United States, [as reported by BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/firefox-now-has-a-free-built-in-vpn-with-50gb-monthly-data-limit/). The company explained it may log whether a connection succeeded or failed, or record aggregate data usage on a given day, but does not track browsing activity.

The VPN is not entirely unique in the browser market. [The Register noted](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/firefox_149/) that Opera and Vivaldi already offer comparable unlimited VPN functionality built into their browsers, though Mozilla's implementation is positioned as a more privacy-respecting alternative aligned with the organization's nonprofit mission.

Beyond the VPN, Firefox 149 introduces split view, allowing users to display two web pages side-by-side within a single window, [according to The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/firefox_149/). The active pane is highlighted with a blue outline, while the inactive pane shows a small indicator with the page's domain and favicon. A dedicated menu provides options to separate, reverse, or close both tabs.

Tab notes, available through Firefox Labs, let users attach short annotations to any open tab for context or task management. The feature is experimental and must be enabled through the browser's settings, [as reported by The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/firefox_149/).

On the security front, the release addresses 46 vulnerabilities, over half rated high severity, including use-after-free flaws and sandbox escapes, [according to BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/firefox-now-has-a-free-built-in-vpn-with-50gb-monthly-data-limit/). Firefox now also automatically blocks notifications and permanently revokes permissions for any website flagged as malicious by SafeBrowsing, adding a layer of defense against phishing and spam-based notification abuse.

For Linux users, Firefox 149 now defaults to XDG portal file dialogs instead of the standard GTK3 versions, [as noted by The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/firefox_149/). This change, which has been optionally available since Firefox 64 in 2018, means the browser will use the system's native file picker, improving consistency across desktop environments.

## What We Don't Know

Mozilla has not announced a timeline for expanding the VPN to regions beyond its initial four markets. The company also has not disclosed whether it plans to offer users the ability to select a routing country, a feature available in most dedicated VPN services but absent from this initial release.

The relationship between the free browser VPN and Mozilla's paid VPN product remains unclear. Whether the free tier is intended to serve as an on-ramp to the commercial subscription or as a standalone offering has not been addressed.

The 50 GB monthly cap raises questions about the service's sustainability model. Mozilla has not disclosed how it plans to fund the proxy infrastructure long-term, particularly if adoption scales beyond initial projections.

## Analysis

Firefox 149 represents a strategic inflection point for Mozilla. After spending much of early 2026 managing fallout from its AI pivot, the organization is now leaning into privacy and productivity features that play to Firefox's traditional strengths. A free VPN built into the browser is a direct appeal to privacy-conscious users who may have been considering alternatives.

The 50 GB limit is generous enough for typical browsing but insufficient for heavy media consumption, which effectively positions the feature as a security tool rather than a streaming workaround. This is a deliberate design choice that may help Mozilla avoid the bandwidth costs and content-licensing complications that a truly unlimited offering would invite.

With the Chromium monoculture continuing to expand, Firefox remains the only major independent browser engine. Updates like this one suggest Mozilla is betting that a combination of privacy credibility and practical utility can sustain its user base in an increasingly consolidated market.
